hero.tagline: North Carolina's small claims court offers a $10,000 cap with a streamlined process for quick resolutions.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in district court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $10,000, you cannot split it into multiple small claims cases. You must either reduce your claim to fit within the limit or file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: North Carolina allows you to file claims related to breaches of contract and money owed up to $10,000. The statute of limitations is generally 3 years for such claims.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of breach to file. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $2,000, and they failed to repay as promised.
claims.notes: The statute of limitations is 3 years. Provide evidence of the agreement and any partial payments.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $5,000 with the understanding it would be repaid in a year, but they haven't paid.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the agreed repayment date. Documentation of the loan and any communications are helpful.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $8,000 and failed to make the agreed payments.
claims.notes: You have 3 years from the date of default to file. Present the original note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $3,000 worth of consulting services, invoiced the client, but haven't received payment.
claims.notes: The statute of limitations is 3 years. Keep copies of the invoice, service agreement, and any correspondence.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $6,000 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: File within 3 years from the last payment or charge. Maintain detailed account statements and payment records.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,500 in unpaid rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file. Provide the lease agreement, payment history, and any notices sent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for goods worth $1,200 with a check that bounced.
claims.notes: After a 30-day notice, you can seek the check amount plus damages. Keep the bounced check and notice sent.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 3x check amount, whichever is less.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $7,000 loan, the primary borrower defaulted, and you had to pay.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of your payment. Present the loan agreement and proof of your payments.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: North Carolina's small claims court handles various consumer disputes up to $10,000, with a 3-year statute of limitations for most claims.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $2,000 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of purchase. Bring the receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $800 but did not perform the agreed tasks satisfactorily.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and evidence of poor service.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $1,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and now refuses to refund or redo the work.
claims.notes: File within 3 years. Bring repair invoices, payment records, and any expert opinions on the faulty repair.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory work by a contractor
claims.example: You paid a contractor $9,000 for home renovations that were poorly executed and incomplete.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file. Present the contract, payment records, and photos of the incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You paid $500 for a DJ for your event, but they never showed up and refused to refund.
claims.notes: File within 3 years. Provide the agreement, payment proof, and any communication with the vendor.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your card for 3 months.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file. Bring cancellation confirmation and bank statements showing the charges.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $7,500 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: File within 3 years. Provide the bill of sale, any warranties, and repair estimates.

claims.name: Misleading advertising leading to a purchase
claims.example: A store advertised a laptop for $1,200 with certain features, but the model you received lacked those features.
claims.notes: You have 4 years to file under the Unfair and Deceptive Trade Practices Act. Bring the advertisement and proof of purchase.
claims.damage_boost: Treble damages and attorney's fees may be awarded.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Claims for property damage up to $10,000 can be filed in North Carolina's small claims court, with a 3-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver caused $4,000 in damage to your car and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the accident. Provide the police report, repair estimates, and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Neighbor caused damage to your property
claims.example: Your neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $2,500 in damage, and they refuse to cover the cost.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file. Bring photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A former tenant left $3,000 worth of damage to your rental unit beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: File within 3 years. Provide the lease agreement, move-in/move-out inspection reports, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,000 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file. Bring photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any witness statements.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your bicycle with a friend, and they returned it with $500 in damage.
claims.notes: File within 3 years. Provide evidence of the property's condition before and after, and any agreements made.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes (money only)
claim_categories.blurb: Monetary disputes between landlords and tenants up to $10,000 can be addressed in small claims court, with a 3-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: Landlords must return the deposit within 30 days. File within 3 years if not returned. Bring the lease, move-out inspection report, and any correspondence.

claims.name: Failure to provide itemized deductions from deposit
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $800 from your deposit but didn't provide an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list within 30 days. File within 3 years if not provided. Bring the lease and any communication.

claims.name: Landlord failed to maintain habitable conditions
claims.example: Your rental had no heating during winter, and the landlord refused to fix it.
claims.notes: Landlords must maintain habitable conditions. File within 3 years. Provide photos, repair requests, and any responses.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your furniture worth $3,000.
claims.notes: Landlords cannot retain tenant's property unlawfully. File within 3 years. Bring evidence of ownership and any communication.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your home.
claims.notes: Self-help evictions are illegal. File within 3 years. Provide the lease, photos of changed locks, and any notices received.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but after early termination, the landlord didn't refund the last month's rent.
claims.notes: Landlords must return unearned rent. File within 3 years. Bring the lease and proof of payments.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,500 in unpaid rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file. Provide the lease agreement, payment history, and any notices sent.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You repaired a broken heater for $600 and deducted it from your rent, but the landlord is disputing this.
claims.notes: Tenants may repair and deduct in certain situations. File within 3 years. Provide repair receipts and communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wages
claim_categories.blurb: Claims related to unpaid wages and employment issues up to $10,000 can be filed in small claims court, with a 2-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $1,500 for hours worked but hasn't paid.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Provide timesheets, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.
claims.damage_boost: Possible liquidated damages equal to unpaid wages.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After leaving your job, your employer didn't provide your final paycheck of $2,000.
claims.notes: Employers must pay final wages by the next regular payday. File within 2 years. Bring employment records and any communication.
claims.damage_boost: Possible liquidated damages equal to unpaid wages.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,500 in sales commissions, but your employer hasn't paid them.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Provide sales records, commission agreements, and any related communication.
claims.damage_boost: Possible liquidated damages equal to unpaid wages.

claims.name: Employer didn't pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but weren't paid the overtime rate, totaling $400.
claims.notes: File within 2 years. Bring timesheets, pay stubs, and any communication regarding overtime.
claims.damage_boost: Possible liquidated damages equal to unpaid wages.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Personal claims up to $10,000, such as minor injuries or property disputes, can be filed in small claims court, with a 3-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store, resulting in $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: File within 3 years of the injury. Provide medical records, bills, and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,500 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file. Provide evidence of ownership and any communication regarding the loan.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: Someone stole your bicycle worth $800, and you have evidence of their possession.
claims.notes: File within 3 years. Provide proof of ownership, police reports, and any evidence of the theft.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Vehicle-related disputes up to $10,000 can be addressed in small claims court, with a 3-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and now refuses to refund or redo the work.
claims.notes: File within 3 years. Bring repair invoices, payment records, and any expert opinions on the faulty repair.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and the towing company charged you $300.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file. Provide photos of the parking spot, towing receipts, and any communication with the towing company.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are handled in small claims court but follow specific procedures distinct from other claims.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Small Claims Court with specific eviction procedures

exclusions.explanation: Disputes involving the title to real estate exceed the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Defamation cases involve complex legal issues and are not suitable for small claims court.
Professional malpractice claims require expert testimony and are beyond the scope of small claims court.
Divorce, child custody, and support cases are handled by family courts, not small claims court.
Issues related to wills and estates are under the jurisdiction of probate courts.
Such claims are governed by federal law and not handled in state small claims courts.
These claims are processed through the state's workers' compensation system, not small claims court.
Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Small claims court cannot grant equitable relief such as injunctions or specific performance.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 3x check amount, whichever is less.
Actual damages are tripled, and attorney's fees may be awarded.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney's fees may be awarded in cases involving unfair and deceptive trade practices.

filing_options: Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability depends on the county; check with the local clerk's office.

forms.name: Petition to Sue/Appeal as an Indigent

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and service fees.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge
Date deposit was due to be returned

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention. For bad check claims, a 30-day notice is required before seeking damages.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: Before filing a claim for a bad check, you must send a written demand for payment and wait 30 days.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but it's advisable to request payment in writing before filing.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days of lease termination when withholding a security deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: For claims under the Unfair and Deceptive Trade Practices Act, no pre-filing demand is required, but documenting the deceptive act is crucial.

forms.name: Complaint for Money Owed
forms.description: Initiates a claim for money owed in small claims court.

forms.name: Complaint in Summary Ejectment
forms.description: Used by landlords to file for eviction proceedings.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal to District Court
forms.description: Filed to appeal a small claims court decision to district court.

forms.name: Petition to Sue/Appeal as an Indigent
forms.description: Request to waive filing fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Motion to Claim Exempt Property
forms.description: Filed by a debtor to claim property exemptions from judgment collection.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff personally delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail
methods.description: Clerk sends the summons and complaint via certified mail to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A private individual authorized to serve legal documents delivers the summons and complaint.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with North Carolina rules and the rules of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be an option, but it requires court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, you may request the court to allow alternative methods of service.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but should appear at the scheduled hearing to present their defense.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the magistrate may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant doesn't appear, the plaintiff must present evidence to support their claim before a default judgment is entered.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: A motion to vacate must be filed within 10 days of the judgment; if denied, an appeal can be filed within 10 days of the denial.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If a counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to district court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys, but it is not required.
hearing.format: Bench trial before a magistrate, typically lasting 30 minutes to an hour.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter through the clerk's office prior to the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or within 10 days
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some counties offer mediation services on the day of the hearing; check with the local clerk's office.

appeals.type_notes: The case is heard anew in district court as if the small claims trial never occurred.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: Default judgments can be appealed within 10 days; the appeal results in a new trial in district court.

notice_of_appeal_form.name: Notice of Appeal to District Court

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on the judgment amount from the date of entry at a rate of 8% per annum.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: North Carolina generally does not allow wage garnishment for civil judgments, except for child support.
methods.description: Wage garnishment is not a viable collection method for most civil judgments in North Carolina.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Not applicable for most civil judgments.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seizing funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: After obtaining a writ of execution, the sheriff can levy the debtor's bank account. The debtor must be notified and has the right to claim exemptions.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account; debtor may claim exemptions.
exemptions: Social Security and other federal benefits

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Placing a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: By docketing the judgment with the clerk of court, a lien is placed on the debtor's real property in that county. The lien must be renewed every 10 years.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property; may not result in immediate payment but secures the debt.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Authorizes the sheriff to seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: After the 10-day appeal period, the creditor can request a writ of execution. The sheriff will attempt to locate and seize non-exempt property for sale.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Depends on the debtor's assets; debtor may claim exemptions.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before the 10-year period expires; the judgment can be renewed once for an additional 10 years.

debtor_exam_form.name: Notice of Rights to Have Exemptions Designated

collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Judgment liens are prioritized based on the date of docketing; earlier liens have higher priority.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can appear without an attorney in small claims court; an owner or employee may represent the business.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a trade name must file a Certificate of Assumed Name with the county.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may have an employee represent them in small claims court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face limitations in enforcing contracts; check specific licensing requirements.

county_variations.detail: Wake County offers e-filing for small claims cases through the eCourts portal.
Mecklenburg County provides on-site mediation services for small claims disputes.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party or using incorrect names can result in dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and address of the defendant before filing; for businesses, check the Secretary of State's records.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations expires leads to automatic dismissal of the case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Confirm the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within the allowed time frame.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to serve the defendant correctly can delay the case or result in dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the prescribed methods of service and ensure proof of service is filed with the court.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you don't respond, you could lose by default.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Address any counterclaims promptly and prepare to defend against them in court.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment doesn't guarantee payment; you must take steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with collection methods and act promptly to enforce the judgment.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking exemptions
pitfalls.what_happens: Debtors can claim certain property as exempt from collection, limiting recovery options.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Understand the debtor's exemption rights and plan collection efforts accordingly.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims monetary limit
recent_changes.description: The jurisdictional limit for small claims court increased from $5,000 to $10,000, allowing more cases to be heard in this forum.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Most small claims cases are scheduled for a hearing within 30 days of filing and are typically resolved at the hearing.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in North Carolina's small claims court is $10,000.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the magistrate may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff, provided the plaintiff presents sufficient evidence.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal the magistrate's decision to district court within 10 days for a new trial.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment is valid for 10 years and can be renewed once for an additional 10 years.

state_specific_notes: North Carolina does not allow wage garnishment for civil judgments, except for child support.
Small claims court is part of the district court division and cases are heard by a magistrate with no jury.
The chief district court judge may assign any case to trial before a magistrate if it meets the small claims criteria.
Either party can appeal the magistrate's decision to district court for a new trial before a judge or jury.
Judgments are valid for 10 years and can be renewed once for an additional 10 years.

interesting_findings: North Carolina's small claims court allows businesses to be represented by non-attorney agents, such as owners or employees.
The state provides a 'Guide & File' tool to help users prepare court documents online for small claims cases.
Some counties offer on-site mediation services to help parties resolve disputes before proceeding to a hearing.
